titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the M. James Slack Papers
titlestmt.author: Sarah Lerner

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This finding aid, which is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0, has a MARC record in Yale's ILS with the following bib number: 15886019.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

conventiondeclaration.citation: translation missing: en.enumerations.resource_finding_aid_description_rules.Describing Archives: A Content Standard

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Saturday July 10, 2021 at 03:01

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract:
Artwork, photographs, and other papers created by M. James Slack and collected by John R. Beeder, circa 1882-2007. A portion of the material relates to Slack's efforts at Acoma Pueblo in New Mexico as part of the Historic American Buildings Survey in 1934.
Included in the collection are Slack's diary for 1937, correspondence (primarily to Rosamond F. Slack as Curator at Fort Garland), and newspaper clippings (primarily relating to Fort Garland).

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: From the estate of John R. Beeder. Purchased from Walkabout Books on the Frederick W. and Carrie S. Beinecke Fund for Western Americana, 2016. Slack family tree and portrait of M. James Slack gift of Amy Beeder, 2016 December.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two series: I. Materials Relating to Acoma Pueblo and the Historic American Buildings Survey, 1934-2007. II. Other Materials, circa 1882-1975.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: M. James Slack (1894-1951) of Denver, Colorado, attended Denver public schools and Manual Training Technical High School before studying art, architecture, and design at the Denver Atelier under architect Burnham Hoyt. He worked as a draftsman for Robert K. Fuller until 1934, when he was hired as a staff photographer on the Historic American Buildings Survey, a project established by the United States National Park Service during the Great Depression. Slack was involved in the work documenting the Acoma Pueblo in New Mexico, specifically.

In 1937, he married Rosamond F. Slack (1910-1989), with whom he had one child. The family lived in Las Cruces, New Mexico, in the 1940s, and ran the El Rio Lindo Curio Shop. In 1950, James Slack was appointed Curator of the grounds and new museum at Fort Garland, Colorado. Following his death from heart disease, Rosamond Slack was appointed Curator (a position she held until she remarried in 1954).

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: John R. Beeder (1932-2015), mining geologist and collector and dealer in Southwestern art and antiques, intended to publish a book on M. James Slack's efforts at Acoma Pueblo in New Mexico, making use of Slack's photographs and unpublished article on the pueblo submitted to National Geographic.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: M. James Slack Papers. Yale Collection of Western Americana, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Beinecke Manuscript Unit Processing Manual.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: Artwork, photographs, and other papers created by M. James Slack and collected by John R. Beeder, circa 1882-2007. A portion of the material relates to Slack's efforts at Acoma Pueblo in New Mexico as part of the Historic American Buildings Survey in 1934.
Included in the collection are Slack's diary for 1937, correspondence (primarily to Rosamond F. Slack as Curator at Fort Garland), and newspaper clippings (primarily relating to Fort Garland).

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The M. James Slack Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

did.unittitle: Materials relating to Acoma Pueblo and the Historic American Buildings Survey

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I consists of 151 photographs taken by M. James Slack of Acoma Pueblo, its inhabitants, and surrounding landscape as part of the Historic American Buildings Survey (HABS) in 1934. Also included are enlargements of 36 of the photographs (some duplicates), amounting to 51 prints postdating Slack's lifetime. Multiple photographs have been annotated by John R. Beeder. Photographs document the Acoma community before it became accessible by road.

Series I also consists of a typescript (15 pages) of Slack's unpublished article titled "The Historic American Building Survey and the Pueblo of Acoma" (in which Slack intended to incorporate 54 of his photographs) and 12 pen-and-ink drawings of Acoma pottery designs. Included is a typescript report and 7 photographs of Mission San Xavier del Bac (included in the HABS).

Present are 57 color photographs (with negatives) likely taken by John R. Beeder of the pueblo, as well as 50 photographs (taken by Slack) sent to Beeder from Victor Hornbein, a draftsman on the HABS Acoma survey.

Also present are two publications relating to the pueblo: Architecture of Acoma Pueblo: the 1934 Historic American Buildings Survey Project by Peter Nabokov (Santa Fe: Ancient City Press, 1986) and Recording a Vanishing Legacy: the Historic American Buildings Survey in New Mexico, 1933-Today by the New Mexico Architectural Foundation (Santa Fe: Museum of New Mexico Press, 2001).
Included in Series I are Beeder's papers relating to his intended publication of a book on Slack and the pueblo.

did.unittitle: The Historic American Building Survey and the Pueblo of Acoma
Photographs of Acoma Pueblo (Enlargements of 1934 photographs) (4 folders)
Photographs taken by M. James Slack and sent to John R. Beeder by Victor Hornbein (2 folders)
Photographs taken by John R. Beeder (4 folders)
Photographs of and report on Mission San Xavier Del Bac
Architecture of Acoma Pueblo: the 1934 Historic American Buildings Survey Project by Peter Nabokov (Santa Fe: Ancient City Press, 1986)
Recording a Vanishing Legacy: the Historic American Buildings Survey in New Mexico, 1933-Today by the New Mexico Architectural Foundation (Santa Fe: Museum of New Mexico Press, 2001)
Materials created by John R. Beeder (5 folders)

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series II is arranged alphabetically by format.
Quoted phrases in parentheses were taken from folders received at time of acquisition.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II consists of artwork (drawings, watercolors, and block prints), photographs, two wooden religious sculptures (also known as santos), and other papers.
Themes represented in Slack's artwork include Indigenous or Southwestern peoples and patterns, Ancient Egypt, the Middle Ages, architecture, interiors, and religion.
Photographs are primarily portraits of Slack and relatives.
Other papers include M. James Slack's diary for 1937, correspondence (primarily to Rosamond F. Slack as Curator at Fort Garland), and newspaper clippings (primarily relating to Fort Garland).

did.unittitle: Artwork (Colorado Engineering Council Medal of Award)
Correspondence (addressed to Rosamond F. Slack) (4 folders)
